Motion For Summary Adjudication Of 1st And 4th Causes Of Action In Second Amended Cross-Complaint
Off Calendar Dec-22-2017 Denied
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Helen Kim 415-388-6368 for Plaintiff/Cross-Defendant in Pro Per.
William Zeiring, Esq. 415-823-9480 for Plaintiff Stephen Roloff and Namo Interactive (US).
Brian Song, Esq. 408-748-3305 of Song & Lee for Defendant/Cross-Complainant Namo Korea.

RULING - A R G U E D. Plaintiff Kim argues. Defendant responds to abandonment.(as fully reported in the reporter's notes) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Cross-Defendant Namo Ineractive Inc. (Namo US)'s Motion For Summary Adjudication Of 1st And 4th Causes Of Action In Second Amended Cross-Complaint.

Cross-Defendants Namo Interactive, Inc., Stephen Roloff and Helene Kim's motion for summary adjudication is granted as to the fourth cause of action for conversion. The undisputed facts show that Namo US did not exert wrongful dominion over the $189,000 and/or Namo Korea was not entitled to immediate possession. In undisputed facts 9-11, Namo Korea admits that it demanded that Namo US deposit the funds in a Namo US account and Namo US complied with that directive.

Cross-Defendants' motion for summary adjudication is denied as to the first cause of action. The May 12, 2015 e-mail provides that "the board decides that Namo gives up US business'" The e-mail is ambiguous and does not clearly show that Namo Korea abandoned its equity interest in Namo US. In addition, paragraphs 34-36 of the S.B. Kim declaration aver that Namo Korea merely planned to close the entity down and no longer fund it. There is a triable issue of material fact as to whether Namo Korea retained an interest in Namo US. Even though Namo Korea ignored the shareholder meeting notice, there is a triable issue of fact regarding whether there was a proper quorum at the April 15, 2016 meeting. California Corporations Code 602 provides that a quorum requires a majority of the shareholders. Namo Korea owned 96% of the outstanding shares; and it is disputed whether that equity interest was abandoned. (SB Kim Decl., Ex. N pg. 3.) If Namo Korea retained its equity interest, the April 15, 2016 meeting was a nullity.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Andrew Cheng, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Vicki Gross, CSR #5525, vgross@sftc.org, Reported. (D302/AC)

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Nov-30-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances: William Ziering telephone number 415-982-4581 for Plaintiffs/Cross-Defendants Stephen Roloff, Helene Kim, Namo Interactive, Inc. and Lexbridge, LLC.
Brian Song of Song & Lee Silicon Valley Law Firm telephone number 408-748-3308 for all Defendants.

The matter was argued. Upon conclusion of argument, the Court modified its tentative ruling as to the 4th and 6th causes of action. Cross-defendants Namo Interactive, Inc. (Namo US), Lexbridge, LLC, Helene Kim, and Stephen Roloff's demurrer to all ten causes of action in the first amended cross-complaint is: 1) overruled as to the first cause of action for an order to hold a shareholder meeting, the seventh cause of action for rescission of void contract, the eighth cause of action for breach of fiduciary duty of lawyer, the ninth cause for violation of the Unfair Competition Law, and the tenth cause of action for goods sold and services provided; 2) sustained with twenty days leave to amend as to the second cause of action for removal of directors and the fifth cause of action for breach of fiduciary duties and 3) sustained without leave to amend as to the third cause of action for writ of mandamus; 4) sustained with twenty days leave to amend as to the fourth cause of action for conversion, and the sixth cause of action for declaratory relief for voiding contract. As to the first cause of action, Namo Korea has adequately alleged a claim for failure to hold an annual shareholders' meeting per Corporations Code 600(c). The second cause of action is deficient because it does not allege that Ms. Kim and Mr. Roloff committed fraudulent or dishonest acts or gross abuse of authority (Corporations Code 304) and Namo Korea is given leave to correct this deficiency if it can do so in good faith. As to the third cause of action, a writ of mandate remedy is not applicable to alleged misconduct committed by private parties that are not acting in the nature of a quasi-governmental organization. As to the fifth cause of action, Namo Korea's request for leave to amend is granted in light of the liberal policy in favor of granting requests for leave to amend. As to the seventh cause of action, cross-defendants' argument based on full performance is disregarded as it was first raised in the reply. Namo Korea has adequately alleged a claim for rescission per section 29(b) of the Exchange Act. As to the eighth cause of action, taking all of the allegations in the first amended complaint as true, Namo Korea has sufficiently alleged a cause of action for breach of fiduciary duty by an attorney. As to the ninth cause of action, Namo Korea has sufficiently alleged a claim for a violation of the UCL. As to the tenth cause of action, the claim for goods and services rendered is sufficiently alleged. The Court will modify the proposed order to reflect its ruling.

Judge: Harold Kahn. Clerk: Rosie Noguera. Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR #5976. (302/HK)

DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Nov-17-2016 Overruled
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances: Attorney William Ziering telephone number 415-982-4581 for all Plaintiffs.
Brian Song of Song & Lee telephone number 408-748-3308 for all Defendants.

The matter was argued. Upon conclusion of argument, the Court confirmed its tentative ruling.
Defendants Namo Korea Ltd., Sang Bae Kim, and Soo Keun Lee's demurrer to all causes of action in the second amended complaint is overruled in its entirety. While it could and should have more clearly alleged, the second amended complaint adequately states which plaintiff or plaintiffs are bringing each cause of action and/or can be determined by the context of each claim. Any uncertainty on this issue can be clarified by communications between counsel or discovery and should not prevent this case being at issue. Plaintiffs' addition of Namo Interactive, Inc. as a plaintiff is permissible since the order on the demurrer to the first amended complaint necessarily contemplated the addition of a new party who had standing to assert the claims that the prior order determined that the individual defendants lacked standing. The addition of the ninth cause of action is permissible since it is based on the same predicate facts as the fifth cause of action, for which leave to amend was granted in the order on the demurrer to the first amended complaint. The context of the sixth cause of action for misappropriation of trade secrets reveals that it is brought on behalf of the corporate plaintiff and thus re-alleging that claim does not run afoul of the order on the demurrer to the first amended complaint. Order signed in open Court.

Judge: Harold Kahn. Clerk: Rosie Noguera. Reporter: Kelley Anne DeYoung, CSR #6780. (302/HK)

Notice Of Hearing And Motion To Dismiss
Off Calendar Nov-17-2016 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances: Attorney William Ziering telephone number 415-982-4581 for all Plaintiffs.
Brian Song of Song & Lee telephone number 408-748-3308 for all Defendants.

The matter was argued. Upon conclusion of argument, the Court confirmed its tentative ruling.
DEFENDANTS SANG KIM, SOO LEE and NAMO INTERACTIVE, INC'S Motion To Dismiss is denied. Defendants have not shown that an attorney-client relationship existed between Namo Korea and plaintiff Helene Kim. Defendants do not describe any legal work performed or legal advice given by Ms. Kim to or for the benefit of Namo Korea. Solin v. O'Melveny & Meyers ((2001) 89 Cal.App.4th 451 is not on point. Unlike Solin, there is nothing in this case that prevents defendants from marshalling all relevant evidence in their defense. In fact, defendants appear to be relying on some of the very same matters in their cross-complaint that they contend in this motion are covered by the attorney-client privilege. Order signed in open Court.

DEFENDANTS SOO LEE AND NAMO KOREA LTD.'S DEMURRER TO FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT is OVERRULED in part and SUSTAINED in part. Second Count: a claim for breach of oral contract is adequately pled; the demurrer is OVERRULED. Third Count: conversion requires that a plaintiff have a right to possession; the demurrer to this count is thus SUSTAINED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND. Fourth Count: fraud is adequately pled; the demurrer is OVERRULED. Fifth Count: plaintiffs' request for leave to amend is GRANTED. Sixth Count: the trade secrets are not alleged to belong to current plaintiffs, so this count is SUSTAINED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND. Seventh & Eighth Counts: defendants' lack-of-standing arguments are meritorious; demurrers are SUSTAINED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND. Vicarious Liability: the demurrer is SUSTAINED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND. Written order is signed and filed in open court.

DEFENDANT SANG KIM'S Motion To Quash Summons is DENIED. Plaintiffs have demonstrated sufficient contacts between SB Kim and California related to the pleaded causes, and Kim has not shown that jurisdiction over him would be unreasonable. (See Magnecomp Corp. v. Athene Co. (1989) 209 Cal.App.3d 526.) Jurisdiction is also proper on the independently sufficient ground that plaintiffs adequately allege tortious conduct by SB Kim directed at California residents. (See Taylor-Rush v. Multitech Corp.
